questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.subsection: 1.8.4 Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
questions.structural_anchor: Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
questions.question: What is one major advantage of the modern periodic table over Mendeleev's periodic table?

options.A: It is based on atomic number, not atomic mass
options.B: It contains more elements in a single row
options.C: It arranges elements by alphabetical order

questions.hint: Mendeleev used atomic mass; modern uses atomic number.
questions.explanation: Modern periodic law (Moseley) uses atomic number, which resolves anomalies like Ar (18) before K (19) despite K having lower mass. Option B is false; C and D are nonsense.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.8.4 Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
source_trace.structural_element: Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: classification of elements is based on the atomic number

explanations_tiered.definition: The modern periodic table arranges elements by increasing atomic number, resolving inconsistencies of Mendeleev's mass-based table.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Atomic number (proton count) is a more fundamental property than atomic mass, giving correct order and periodic trends.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Mendeleev used weight; modern uses ID number (protons). Weight sometimes caused wrong order, but ID number never does.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Example: Ar (Z=18, mass 40) and K (Z=19, mass 39). Mass-based table would put K before Ar; atomic number-based puts Ar before K, matching chemical properties.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Moseley's X-ray spectroscopy established atomic number as the basis, making the periodic law a function of nuclear charge, not atomic mass.
reasoning_steps: Recall Mendeleev's table used atomic mass.
misconceptions: Some think Mendeleev's table was incorrect — it was largely correct but had a few swapped elements.
Atomic number is not the same as mass number.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Modern table uses Z (atomic number), fixing mass anomalies.

glossary_entry.definition: Based on atomic number, giving correct element order and explaining periodic trends.

flashcard.front: What improvement does modern periodic table have over Mendeleev's?
flashcard.back: Based on atomic number, not atomic mass.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is a key advantage of modern periodic table?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Periodic table arranged by increasing atomic number.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Write the advantages of the periodic classification of elements
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Modern table based on atomic number, fixing Mendeleev's anomalies.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.subsection: 1.8.4 Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
questions.structural_anchor: Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
questions.question: How does the periodic table handle isotopes of the same element?

options.A: Isotopes are placed in separate positions
options.B: Isotopes are averaged into one position
options.C: Isotopes are omitted from the table
options.D: Isotopes are listed in the same group but different periods

questions.hint: All isotopes of an element have the same atomic number.
questions.explanation: Isotopes have the same atomic number (Z), so they occupy the same position. The atomic mass is the weighted average of isotopic masses, appearing as the element's atomic weight.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.8.4 Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
source_trace.structural_element: Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: Isotopes are averaged in one place, as the classification is on the basis of atomic number

explanations_tiered.definition: Isotopes share the same atomic number, so they occupy the same position in the periodic table; the atomic mass shown is a weighted average.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Because chemical properties depend on electron configuration, all isotopes of an element behave similarly and are placed together.
explanations_tiered.beginner: All carbon atoms, whether carbon-12 or carbon-14, go in the same box on the periodic table.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Chlorine-35 and chlorine-37 are not separate entries; the table lists Cl with atomic mass 35.45 — the average of its isotopes.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Atomic number, not mass, determines position. Thus, even though isotopic masses differ, the element's position is fixed, and the reported atomic mass is the average of natural abundances.
misconceptions: Some think each isotope has its own box — only one box per element.
Atomic mass listed is not a single isotope mass but an average.

glossary_entry.definition: All isotopes of an element occupy one position because they share atomic number.

flashcard.front: Where are isotopes placed on periodic table?
flashcard.back: Same position; atomic mass is an average.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How are isotopes represented in periodic table?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: One position; atomic mass is average of isotopes.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Periodic table lists weighted average atomic mass for all isotopes.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain atomic mass is not a whole number usually.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain advantages of periodic classification regarding isotopes
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Isotopes occupy same position because they share atomic number.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.subsection: 1.8.4 Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
questions.structural_anchor: Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
questions.question: Where are lanthanides and actinides placed in the modern periodic table?

options.A: Within the main body of the periodic table
options.B: Separately at the bottom of the periodic table
options.C: In a single column at the left side
options.D: They are not included in the periodic table

questions.hint: These f-block elements are usually placed below the main table.
questions.explanation: Lanthanides and actinides (f-block) are placed separately below the main table to keep the table's width manageable. They belong to periods 6 and 7 but are extracted.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.8.4 Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
source_trace.structural_element: Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: The lanthanides and actinides are placed separately at the bottom of the periodic table

explanations_tiered.definition: Lanthanides (4f) and actinides (5f) are placed in two rows below the main periodic table for compactness.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If placed in the main body, period 6 would have 32 elements and period 7 similarly, making the table extremely wide.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The two rows at the very bottom of the periodic table are the lanthanides and actinides.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Lanthanides (Ce–Lu) and actinides (Th–Lr) are f-block elements, usually shown as separate rows below periods 6 and 7.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This placement is a convenience for printing; electronically, they belong in periods 6 and 7, groups 3. Their electron configurations involve filling 4f and 5f subshells.
reasoning_steps: Recall lanthanides and actinides are f-block.
They are placed below main table for space.
misconceptions: Some think they are not part of periods 6/7 — they are, just extracted.
Not all f-block elements are radioactive (lanthanides are mostly stable).

glossary_entry.definition: f-block elements placed separately below the main periodic table.
glossary_entry.example_equation: Ce to Lu (4f), Th to Lr (5f)

flashcard.front: Where are lanthanides on periodic table?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Where are lanthanides placed in periodic table?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Placed below main table for compactness.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain why they are extracted (wide table).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain placement of lanthanides and actinides
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Lanthanides and actinides are placed separately at the bottom.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.subsection: 1.8.4 Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
questions.structural_anchor: Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
questions.question: The periodic table allows scientists to predict properties of undiscovered elements because ______.

options.A: properties repeat periodically with atomic number
options.B: elements are arranged by alphabetical order

questions.hint: Periodicity means gaps can be filled by analogy.
questions.explanation: Periodic law states that properties repeat periodically with atomic number. Mendeleev predicted properties of then-unknown elements (e.g., germanium) using trends. Options B, C, D are false.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.8.4 Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
source_trace.structural_element: Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: The table is a simple, systematic and an easy way of remembering the properties of various elements

explanations_tiered.definition: The periodic law allows prediction of unknown elements' properties based on their position in the table.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Mendeleev left gaps and accurately predicted properties of scandium, gallium, and germanium before their discovery.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like filling a puzzle: if you see a missing piece, you can guess its shape and color from the pieces around it.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Eka-silicon (predicted by Mendeleev) had properties remarkably close to germanium discovered later, confirming the predictive power.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Periodicity arises from repeating electron configurations. Therefore, the element in a specific group will resemble others in that group, enabling prediction.
reasoning_steps: Recall periodic law: properties repeat with Z.
Gaps in table correspond to undiscovered elements.
misconceptions: Some think all elements were known when table was made — many were not.
Predictions are not random; they rely on periodic trends.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Periodic table predicts undiscovered elements via periodicity.

glossary_entry.definition: Ability to estimate properties of unknown elements from periodic trends.

flashcard.front: How can periodic table predict unknown elements?
flashcard.back: Periodic repetition of properties with atomic number.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why can periodic table predict unknown elements?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Properties repeat periodically with atomic number.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Ability to forecast properties via periodic trends.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Periodicity with atomic number allows prediction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.question: Which ancient Greek philosopher proposed that matter consists of tiny, indivisible particles called "atomos"?
questions.explanation: Democritus (460–370 BC) suggested that dividing matter leads to indivisible particles called atomos. Aristotle and Plato believed matter is continuous.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.content_anchor: Democritus ... suggested ... tiny, indestructible particles called atomos

explanations_tiered.definition: Democritus proposed that matter is composed of indivisible particles called atomos (atoms).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: His ideas were philosophical, not experimental, but laid groundwork for later atomic theory.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Democritus said you can't keep cutting matter forever — eventually you reach a tiny piece you can't cut, an atom.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Unlike Aristotle and Plato who believed matter is continuous, Democritus argued for discrete, indivisible particles.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Though Democritus had no experimental evidence, his atomos concept anticipated Dalton's atomic theory by over 2000 years.
reasoning_steps: Recall Greek philosophers: Aristotle/Plato believed continuous matter.
misconceptions: Some think Aristotle proposed atoms — he opposed the idea.
Democritus's atoms were not based on experiments.

glossary_entry.definition: Greek word for indivisible, used by Democritus for basic particles of matter.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Who first proposed that matter is made of indivisible particles?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Ancient Greek philosopher who proposed atomos.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with Aristotle's continuous matter view.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compare views of different philosophers on the nature of matter
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Democritus proposed atomos (indivisible particles).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.3 Early Experiments to Characterize the Atom
questions.subsection: 1.3.2 Radioactivity and the Discovery of the Nucleus
questions.question: Which type of radioactive emission has the highest penetrating power but no charge?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are high-energy electromagnetic radiation with no charge and very high penetrating power (requires thick lead or concrete to stop). Alpha particles (helium nuclei) have low penetration; beta particles (electrons) have medium penetration.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.3 Early Experiments to Characterize the Atom
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.3.2 Radioactivity and the Discovery of the Nucleus
source_trace.content_anchor: Gamma rays ... have no charge and are not affected by an external electric or magnetic field

explanations_tiered.definition: Gamma rays are high-energy photons with no mass or charge, capable of penetrating deeply into matter.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Penetration order: γ > β > α. Gamma rays require thick lead or concrete for shielding.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Gamma rays are like super-powerful X-rays that can go through thick walls.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Alpha stopped by paper, beta by aluminum, gamma by several cm of lead. Gamma has no charge and highest energy.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Gamma rays arise from nuclear de-excitation, with energies typically 0.1–10 MeV. Their high penetration is due to lack of charge and small interaction cross-section.
misconceptions: Some think alpha has highest penetration — false; alpha stopped by paper.
Gamma rays are not particles; they are EM radiation.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: γ rays: highest penetration, no charge.

glossary_entry.definition: High-energy electromagnetic radiation from nuclear decay, no charge, highest penetrating power.

flashcard.front: Which radiation has highest penetration and no charge?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: High-energy EM radiation, no charge, highest penetration.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Penetrating, neutral EM radiation from nucleus.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare α, β, γ penetrating power.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe common types of radioactive emissions
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: Radioactivity and the Discovery of the Nucleus
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Gamma rays have no charge and highest penetrating power.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.6 The Quantum Mechanical Model of the Atom
questions.question: For an electron in a 3d orbital, what are the values of n, ℓ, and mℓ?
questions.hint: d orbitals have ℓ=2, and n is the number before the letter.
questions.explanation: 3d: n=3, ℓ=2 (d subshell). mℓ ranges from -ℓ to +ℓ, so -2,-1,0,+1,+2. Option B is for p, C for s, D has n=4.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.6 The Quantum Mechanical Model of the Atom

explanations_tiered.definition: For a 3d orbital, principal quantum number n=3, angular momentum ℓ=2 (d), magnetic mℓ can be -2,-1,0,+1,+2.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The number gives n; the letter maps to ℓ (s=0, p=1, d=2, f=3). mℓ specifies which orbital within the subshell.
explanations_tiered.beginner: 3d means 'level 3, d-shaped orbital'. d orbitals have ℓ=2 and come in five orientations (mℓ values).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: ℓ=0→s (1 orbital, mℓ=0); ℓ=1→p (3 orbitals, mℓ=-1,0,+1); ℓ=2→d (5 orbitals, mℓ=-2,-1,0,+1,+2).
explanations_tiered.advanced: For a given n, ℓ ranges from 0 to n-1. For n=3, allowed ℓ=0,1,2. ℓ=2 corresponds to d, with five mℓ values representing five orbitals.
worked_solution: Step 2: Identify ℓ: s=0, p=1, d=2 → ℓ=2.
misconceptions: Some think 3d means n=3, d=1 — wrong; d corresponds to ℓ=2.
mℓ is not a single value for the subshell; it's a set.

flashcard.front: What are n, ℓ, and possible mℓ for a 3d orbital?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Give quantum numbers for a 3d orbital.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: d orbital with principal quantum number 3.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain quantum numbers and their allowed values

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.subsection: 1.7.1 Ground State Electronic Configuration of the Elements
questions.structural_anchor: Ground State Electronic Configuration of the Elements
questions.question: Why is the ground state electron configuration of chromium [Ar] 4s¹ 3d⁵ instead of [Ar] 4s² 3d⁴?

options.A: Half-filled d subshell has extra stability
options.D: Chromium has a different atomic number

questions.hint: A half-filled set of d orbitals is especially stable.
questions.explanation: Chromium has an exceptional configuration because a half-filled 3d⁵ subshell (one electron in each of the five d orbitals) is lower in energy than 3d⁴. This extra stability comes from exchange energy and symmetry.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.7.1 Ground State Electronic Configuration of the Elements
source_trace.structural_element: Ground State Electronic Configuration of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: half-filled 3d subshell of chromium lends a special stability

explanations_tiered.definition: Chromium's ground state is [Ar]4s¹3d⁵ because a half-filled d subshell provides extra stability due to exchange energy.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Cr (Z=24) expected [Ar]4s²3d⁴, but observed [Ar]4s¹3d⁵. The 3d⁵ configuration has one electron in each d orbital (Hund's rule) and lower total energy.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Chromium is happier with five unpaired d electrons (one in each d orbital) than with four, even though it loses one from the 4s orbital.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Half-filled subshells (d⁵, f⁷) and fully filled subshells (d¹⁰, f¹⁴) have extra stability due to symmetry and exchange energy. Chromium and copper are classic examples.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The energy difference between 4s and 3d is small. For Cr, the exchange energy gained by having five parallel spins in 3d outweighs the cost of promoting an electron from 4s to 3d.
reasoning_steps: Reason: half-filled 3d subshell is especially stable.
misconceptions: Some think 4s always fills before 3d even in exceptions — not true; exceptions occur.
Not all transition metals have exceptions; only Cr, Cu, and a few others.

glossary_entry.definition: Cr has [Ar]4s¹3d⁵ instead of [Ar]4s²3d⁴ due to half-filled d subshell stability.

flashcard.front: What is chromium's ground state electron configuration? Why?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Electronic stability of d⁵ and f⁷ configurations.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Write ground state electron configurations, including exceptions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Half-filled d subshell gives extra stability.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.question: What is effective nuclear charge (Z_eff)?

options.A: The net positive charge experienced by an electron after accounting for shielding
options.C: The number of protons minus the number of neutrons
options.D: The charge of the nucleus divided by atomic radius

questions.hint: Shielding reduces the pull felt by outer electrons.
questions.explanation: Z_eff = Z (protons) - S (shielding constant). It is the net charge that an electron actually experiences. Option B ignores shielding; C and D are incorrect.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: effective nuclear charge (Z_eff) the nuclear charge an electron actually experiences

explanations_tiered.definition: Effective nuclear charge is the net positive charge from the nucleus that an electron feels after accounting for shielding by inner electrons.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Z_eff increases across a period (poor shielding) but is roughly constant down a group (more shielding).
explanations_tiered.beginner: It's the 'real pull' an electron feels after inner electrons block some of the nucleus's pull.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Z_eff = Z - S. For valence electrons, S is number of core electrons (approx). Across a period, Z increases but S stays similar, so Z_eff increases.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Slater's rules provide approximate S values. Z_eff explains trends: higher Z_eff → smaller radius, higher IE, higher electronegativity.
reasoning_steps: Z_eff is net charge felt by electron.
misconceptions: Some think Z_eff = Z — shielding is ignored.
Shielding not perfect; Z_eff is always less than Z for valence electrons.

glossary_entry.definition: Net positive charge experienced by an electron, Z_eff = Z - S.

flashcard.back: Net nuclear charge felt by an electron after shielding.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Z_eff = Z - S, net charge after shielding.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Net charge experienced by an electron.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the effect of effective nuclear charge
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Z_eff is net charge after shielding.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
questions.section: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
questions.question: Which factor primarily causes the increase in ionization energy from left to right across a period?
questions.hint: More protons attract electrons more strongly.
questions.explanation: Across a period, Z_eff increases (more protons, similar shielding), pulling electrons closer and making them harder to remove. Option A is opposite (radius decreases); B is false (Z_eff increases); D is false (shielding constant).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: ATOMIC STRUCTURE AND PERIODIC PROPERTIES OF THE ELEMENTS
source_trace.section_anchor: 1.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements
source_trace.content_anchor: as we move across a period, Z_eff generally increases so atomic radii become smaller. As a result, the attraction between the nucleus and the outer electrons increases

explanations_tiered.definition: Ionization energy increases across a period because Z_eff increases, making electrons harder to remove.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Higher Z_eff → stronger attraction between nucleus and valence electrons → requires more energy to remove an electron.
explanations_tiered.beginner: As you move right, the nucleus gets more positive, so it holds electrons tighter. It's harder to steal an electron.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In Period 3, Na (Z_eff≈2.2) has low IE; Cl (Z_eff≈6.1) has high IE. More protons without more shells increases pull.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Z_eff increases because each added proton increases Z, but shielding (S) increases only slightly (electrons added to same shell). Net effect: Z_eff rises, decreasing atomic radius and increasing IE.
reasoning_steps: Recall IE increases left to right.
misconceptions: Some think increasing atomic radius increases IE — false; radius decreases, IE increases.
Shielding does not increase significantly across a period.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: IE ↑ across period due to Z_eff ↑.

glossary_entry.definition: Increase in Z_eff across a period leads to higher IE.

flashcard.front: Why does IE increase across a period?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What causes IE to increase across a period?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Link Z_eff to atomic radius decrease.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain ionization energy trend across a period
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Increasing effective nuclear charge raises ionization energy.
